title: Cat Hiding Under the Bed: Causes and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Is your cat hiding under the bed? Learn why cats hide, when it becomes an emergency, and how to triage your pet's health at home.
content_html:
What is it?

Hiding is a natural feline defense mechanism. When a cat retreats under a bed and refuses to emerge, they are attempting to create a safe boundary between themselves and a perceived threat. This behavior can be triggered by external stressors like loud noises or new visitors, but it is also a primary way cats cope with physical pain, nausea, or systemic illness.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: Medium. While hiding due to a temporary scare is common, persistent hiding is a red flag. If your cat remains under the bed for more than 24 hours, misses more than two meals, or is unable to use the litter box, it requires veterinary attention. You should seek immediate care if the hiding is accompanied by open-mouth breathing, vocalizing in pain, or visible bleeding.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Because cats are masters at masking illness, a photo or video of your cat in their hiding spot can be incredibly helpful for a veterinary professional. Capturing their resting posture, the position of their ears, and their facial expressions allows a vet to assess for signs of clinical pain (the Feline Grimace Scale) without the adrenaline of a clinic visit masking those symptoms.

faqs.question: Should I pull my cat out from under the bed?
faqs.answer: Unless there is an immediate life-threatening danger, it is best not to force your cat out, as this can increase their stress and may lead to defensive biting or scratching. Instead, try to lure them with strong-smelling food or wait for them to emerge on their own.

faqs.question: How can I tell if my cat is hiding because they are sick?
faqs.answer: Look for subtle signs such as a hunched posture, squinted eyes, or a lack of interest in their favorite treats. If they are also neglecting their grooming or have stopped using the litter box, the cause is likely medical rather than behavioral.

faqs.question: What are common stressors that cause hiding?
faqs.answer: Common triggers include home renovations, new pets or family members, loud thunderstorms, or even changes in your own schedule. If no environmental changes have occurred, assume a medical cause until proven otherwise.
